axis: hexa runtime persistent-pipe primitive — design doc unlocking ω-audio-2 60× chain target
trigger: ω-audio-2 Phase 3 measured 8-10× chain (vs 60× design target); residual floor is per-call fork+FIFO; cannot reduce without runtime change

predecessors.worker_design: /Users/ghost/core/anima/state/omega_audio_2_persistent_worker_design.json (Q3 explicitly defers a hexa-runtime persistent-pipe primitive)
predecessors.current_consumer_shim: /Users/ghost/core/anima/anima-speak/_audio_worker_call.sh (133 lines bash bridge that this primitive replaces)

current_state_summary.exec_signature: fn exec(cmd: string) -> string  // popen('r'); one-shot; reads child stdout to EOF; pclose discards exit
current_state_summary.exec_with_status_signature: fn exec_with_status(cmd: string) -> [string, int]  // popen + pclose; returns [stdout, exit_code]
current_state_summary.exec_capture_signature: fn exec_capture(cmd: string) -> [stdout, stderr, exit_code]  // pipe()+fork()+execvp('/bin/sh', '-c', cmd); read both pipes to EOF; waitpid; one-shot
current_state_summary.exec_stream_signature: fn exec_stream(cmd: string, on_line: fn(string)) -> int  // popen('r'); fgets() loop invoking hexa callback per line; returns exit; still one-shot, write-side not exposed
current_state_summary.exec_replace_signature: fn exec_replace(cmd: string)  // execvp() in current process; no return path
current_state_summary.proc_spawn_supervised: stdlib/proc.hexa fire-and-forget detached spawn for long-running daemons (lease+TTL). NOT bidirectional; no fd handle returned; cannot be repurposed
current_state_summary.key_observation: All five exec* surfaces are popen-class one-shots. No primitive returns a live (pid, stdin_fd, stdout_fd) handle. exec_capture is the closest in mechanics (it already does pipe()+fork()+execvp() rather than popen()) and is the natural code donor for the new primitive.

measured_floor_attribution.per_link_floor_ms_observed: 67-75 (observed in vocal_only and breath_only worker means)
measured_floor_attribution.links_per_chain: 2 (vocal source + pipe filter — each is one _run_py call → one _audio_worker_call.sh invocation)

floor_components_per_call_ms./bin/cat_or_/bin/base64_inside_caller_pa: ~30-40 (base64 of 2-3KB python source via shell pipeline)

what_a_persistent_pipe_eliminates: the entire /bin/cat | /bin/base64 caller-side encoding pipeline (~30-40ms × 1) — replaced by hexa-side string passthrough (or in-runtime base64 if we keep it for the protocol)
what_remains_after: actual read(fd) syscall + worker line-readline + JSON encode: ~1ms
subtotal: ~7-8ms per call → ~15ms per chain link → ~30ms per 2-link chain → roughly 40× over 1177ms baseline; closer to 60× when scipy lfilter cache stays warm in worker
measured_floor_attribution.projected_speedup_if_implemented: 30-50× chain warm (vs current 8-10×); approaches but may not fully reach 60× target — remaining gap is WAV I/O and JSON framing, addressable in a follow-up via shared-mem or skipping JSON for hot paths

api_surface_proposal.rationale: Iterator (b) reads cleanly but requires hexa-side method dispatch on a runtime opaque type — hexa's TAG system has TAG_VALSTRUCT but no native 'host-resource handle' tag, and adding one is bigger than the primitive itself. Implicit-cache (c) hides the lifecycle (when does the cached child die?), forces a single cmd→child mapping (no parallel workers for the same cmd), and makes error recovery ambiguous. Handle-based (a) maps 1:1 onto the existing exec_capture C pattern (pid + two pipes), uses the existing TAG_INT to carry the handle id (mirroring how POSIX file descriptors are exposed in many runtimes), and composes naturally with try/catch for error flow. Multiple handles trivially supported.

signatures.spawn: fn spawn(cmd: string) -> int  // returns handle id ≥ 0, or -1 on spawn failure (errno surfaced via exec_errno())
signatures.spawn_argv: fn spawn_argv(argv: array<string>) -> int  // direct execvp, no /bin/sh — same security posture as exec_argv
signatures.send: fn send(h: int, payload: string) -> int  // write(stdin_fd, payload); returns bytes written, or -1 on EPIPE / invalid handle
signatures.send_line: fn send_line(h: int, payload: string) -> int  // convenience: send(h, payload + '\n')
signatures.recv_line: fn recv_line(h: int, timeout_ms: int) -> string  // read until '\n' or timeout; returns line WITH trailing newline (matches exec_stream); empty string on EOF or timeout — disambiguate via recv_status(h)
signatures.recv_status: fn recv_status(h: int) -> int  // 0 = ok, 1 = timeout, 2 = EOF/child-died, 3 = invalid handle — checked after recv_line returns empty
signatures.alive: fn alive(h: int) -> bool  // kill(pid, 0) == 0 && handle slot still valid
signatures.close: fn close(h: int) -> int  // close(stdin_fd); close(stdout_fd); SIGTERM pid; waitpid w/ 100ms grace; SIGKILL if needed; returns child exit code
signatures.set_nonblock: fn set_nonblock(h: int, on: bool) -> bool  // fcntl O_NONBLOCK on stdout_fd; lets recv_line() poll without thread

api_surface_proposal.namespace_proposal: spawn / send / send_line / recv_line / recv_status / alive / close — all bare names in the hexa builtin table, mirroring exec / exec_capture / exec_stream. Avoids a 'child.' prefix to keep tab-stops cheap and match existing surface vocabulary.
api_surface_proposal.type_safety_notes: Handle is a plain int. Misuse (calling send on a closed handle) returns -1 + sets exec_errno; does not crash the runtime. Closed handle ids are NOT recycled within a process lifetime to make use-after-close detectable; handle-table grows by realloc when full (start at 16 slots, grow ×2 to 1024 cap = file-descriptor limit-aware).
api_surface_proposal.parallelism: Multiple persistent children supported (each gets a fresh handle). v1 is strictly synchronous per handle (one in-flight request); concurrency across handles is the user's problem (they pick which handle to send/recv). v2 could add recv_select(handles: array<int>, timeout_ms: int) -> int returning the index of the first ready handle.
api_surface_proposal.error_model_alignment: Matches exec_with_status / exec_capture which return arrays carrying exit codes rather than throwing. spawn returning -1 is the same convention as POSIX open() returning -1; hexa code can branch with `if h < 0 { ... }` symmetrically with how exec_with_status checks the second array element.

lifecycle_semantics.spawn_creates: fork() + 2× pipe() (stdin and stdout); child sets dup2() to wire pipes to fd 0 / fd 1, closes parent ends, execvp()s the command. Stderr inherits parent stderr (matching exec_capture's split would double the fd cost; we keep stderr inherited because for the worker use case stderr is logging).
child_dies_when: explicit close(h) — SIGTERM with 100ms grace, then SIGKILL, then waitpid
parent (hexa interp) exits cleanly — atexit handler closes all live handles in handle-table order (SIGTERM then SIGKILL after 100ms grace)

parent killed (SIGKILL) — no atexit runs; child becomes orphan but: (i) child likely sees EOF on stdin once kernel reaps the parent and reaps its pipe write-end → child reads 0 bytes → exits; (ii) anima_audio_worker.py specifically polls _parent_alive() every 5s as a backstop; (iii) if child does neither it leaks until OS reboot — accepted risk, matches current bash-shim behavior

child crashes — next send() returns -1 with EPIPE; next recv_line() returns '' with recv_status() = 2 (EOF/child-died); handle is auto-marked dead; close() is still required to free the slot and reap the zombie
lifecycle_semantics.no_idle_timeout_in_primitive: Idle timeout is policy, not mechanism. Push to user code — the worker python already has IDLE_TIMEOUT_S=300; the hexa side can call alive(h) opportunistically and respawn. Putting it in the primitive bakes a magic number into the runtime.
lifecycle_semantics.max_concurrent_handles: soft cap = min(getrlimit(RLIMIT_NOFILE)/4, 1024). Each handle eats 2 fds (stdin pipe, stdout pipe). Spawn returns -1 when cap hit; exec_errno() surfaces EMFILE.
lifecycle_semantics.zombie_avoidance: close() always waitpid()s. atexit handler also waitpid()s. SIGCHLD is NOT installed (would conflict with user code that sets handlers); we rely on close() being called or atexit running.

error_model.spawn_fail: returns -1; sets thread-local exec_errno (errno from fork/execvp/pipe). Hexa code: `let h = spawn(cmd); if h < 0 { eprintln('spawn failed: ' + to_string(exec_errno())) }`
error_model.send_fail: returns -1; common causes: EPIPE (child closed stdin), EBADF (handle closed/invalid). Hexa convention: `let n = send(h, msg); if n < 0 { /* respawn or bail */ }`
error_model.recv_timeout: recv_line returns ''; recv_status() returns 1. Caller distinguishes timeout from EOF by calling recv_status() before checking empty string.
error_model.recv_eof_child_died: recv_line returns ''; recv_status() returns 2. waitpid is performed inside recv_line on detecting EOF so the exit code is captured into the handle slot for the eventual close() to retrieve.
error_model.invalid_handle: any op on h that is negative, never-spawned, or already-closed: returns -1 (send/close) or '' with recv_status=3 (recv).
error_model.no_throws: primitive never throws. Matches exec_with_status / exec_capture convention. Throwing requires hexa try/catch frames in every call site, which the audio worker call sites are not currently structured for.

implementation_sketch_runtime_c.new_struct: typedef struct { pid_t pid; int stdin_fd; int stdout_fd; int state; /* 0=free,1=live,2=dead-pending-close */ int last_exit; int last_recv_status; } HexaChild;
implementation_sketch_runtime_c.handle_table: static HexaChild* g_children = NULL; static int g_children_cap = 0; static int g_children_count = 0; — array indexed by handle id, grown via realloc with ×2 policy starting at 16. Free slots are NOT reused within a process (monotonic id counter g_next_handle) to keep use-after-close detectable. Optional: for table compaction beyond 1024 slots, gate with a HEXA_CHILD_RECYCLE_HANDLES env var (default off).
implementation_sketch_runtime_c.hexa_full_dispatch: Add cases in the builtin dispatcher (see hexa_full.hexa around line 10219 for the exec_capture pattern). Each new builtin gets a TAG_FN shim (hx_spawn, hx_send, hx_recv_line, hx_close, hx_alive) registered the same way hx_exec_capture is at runtime.c:6990. Estimated ~6 new bt-73 shims + ~6 dispatch arms (~80 LOC in hexa_full.hexa).

loc_estimate.runtime_c_new: ~250 LOC (struct + table + spawn/send/recv/close/alive + atexit + exec_errno helper)
loc_estimate.shims_new: ~30 LOC (TAG_FN registration block, mirroring _hexa_init_exec_sha_fn_shims at runtime.c:6975)
loc_estimate.selftest_new: ~100 LOC (hexa-side test that spawns `cat`, sends 3 lines, recvs them, closes; spawns broken child, asserts -1; spawns slow child, asserts timeout)
loc_estimate.total: ~460 LOC (within the 'small-medium runtime feature' budget; comparable to exec_capture's footprint)

existing_tooling_overlap.stdlib_proc_hexa: fire-and-forget supervised spawn with lease+TTL — does NOT return a bidirectional handle. Strictly additive; the new primitive does not subsume proc.hexa (different use case: proc.hexa is for daemons we want to outlive the parent; spawn() is for child workers that die with us).
existing_tooling_overlap.exec_capture_donor: exec_capture (runtime.c:6010) already does pipe()+fork()+execvp() + dual-pipe drain + waitpid. spawn() reuses the same fork/dup2/execvp pattern — about 60% of the C is copy-able with edits. The only NEW mechanics are: (i) keeping the pipes open after fork instead of draining-then-closing, (ii) the handle table, (iii) write-side exposure.
existing_tooling_overlap.exec_stream_pattern: exec_stream installs a hexa callback per line — useful precedent for streaming output, but still popen-based and one-shot. The new spawn/recv_line model is more flexible because the hexa caller drives the read loop instead of yielding to a callback.
existing_tooling_overlap.no_subsumption_needed: All three (proc.hexa, exec_capture, exec_stream) keep their existing roles. spawn() is strictly additive.

/Users/ghost/core/hexa-lang/self/hexa_full.hexa — add ~80 LOC (6 builtin dispatch arms in the same block as exec_capture at line 10219; mirror its hx_<name>(args[...]) shim-call pattern).
/Users/ghost/core/hexa-lang/self/main.hexa — likely no change (spawn is a builtin, not a syntax keyword).

/Users/ghost/core/hexa-lang/stdlib/proc.hexa — optional: add a thin wrapper `proc_spawn_pipe(cmd)` that delegates to spawn() so the convention 'process primitives live in stdlib/proc' holds. Not load-bearing.

/Users/ghost/core/hexa-lang/stdlib/anima_audio_helpers.hexa (NEW, optional) — ~50 LOC convenience wrapper that owns the worker handle, encodes/decodes JSON, and exposes anima_audio_worker_call(code) -> bool to the consumer. Centralizes the protocol so anima_audio.hexa doesn't bake in NDJSON details.

codesign hexa_interp (line 19; required on darwin for spawn syscall in some sandbox modes)
image rebake (the dispatcher image, if container-based hexa_real is used) — same dance as ω-runner-4 cycle this session
container restart if any agents hold an old hexa_interp open

risk_classes.scenario: spawn() succeeds but caller never close()s and hexa process keeps running (long-lived agent loop). 2 fds per leaked handle; hits RLIMIT_NOFILE (default 256 on darwin) at ~125 leaks.
risk_classes.mitigation: atexit cleanup catches process-exit case; soft cap on max handles; document that close() is mandatory; consider a `with_spawn(cmd, fn)` higher-order helper in stdlib that auto-closes.

risk_classes.scenario: child exits but caller never close()s → defunct process accumulates. Mitigated by recv_line auto-waitpid on EOF detection; remaining gap is when caller stops calling recv at all.
risk_classes.mitigation: atexit reaps everything; optional periodic reap inside hexa_spawn() that walks the table calling waitpid(pid, &st, WNOHANG) on dead-marked slots.

risk_classes.scenario: any .hexa script can spawn arbitrary subprocesses with the same trust as the hexa runtime. SAME risk as exec/exec_capture today — no escalation.
risk_classes.mitigation: no new privilege; document; if needed add HEXA_DISABLE_SPAWN env var that makes spawn() always return -1 (matches existing HEXA_DISABLE_EXEC pattern if any).

risk_classes.scenario: BSD vs Linux differ in PIPE_BUF (512 vs 4096) and select() fd_set max (1024 hard limit on linux without poll). For audio-worker traffic (sub-256-byte JSON) and ≤1024 children, both fit comfortably.
risk_classes.mitigation: use poll() instead of select() if implementation is straightforward; pipe writes stay <PIPE_BUF for atomicity (worker JSON requests are small).

risk_classes.class: deadlock — worker writes >PIPE_BUF before hexa reads
risk_classes.scenario: if worker emits a long line (>4KB on darwin) it blocks on write; hexa is blocked in send() waiting for ack. Doesn't apply to anima_audio (responses are <256 bytes) but bites a future LSP child whose responses can be MB-sized.
risk_classes.mitigation: v1 doc says 'recv_line for line-delimited protocols only; for bulk data, use exec_capture or a separate file channel'; v2 add recv_chunk(h, max_bytes) that drains in pieces.

risk_classes.scenario: if hexa user code installs SIGCHLD handler, our waitpid() inside recv_line / close races with it.
risk_classes.mitigation: do not install SIGCHLD; document; v2 could use signalfd() on linux for race-free child reaping.

risk_classes.scenario: hypothetical if we recycle handle ids — we explicitly DO NOT, so this is moot in v1.
risk_classes.mitigation: monotonic handle counter; slot reuse only behind opt-in env var.

blast_assessment.rollback_strategy: feature is purely additive in runtime.c and hexa_full.hexa; revert via `git revert <commit>`; no on-disk state to clean. Worker .py and bash shim continue to work unchanged during rollout (anima_audio.hexa keeps WORKER_CALL_SH path until Phase B explicitly cuts over).

rollout_plan.phase_A: Implement primitive in runtime.c + hexa_full.hexa dispatch + 6 TAG_FN shims; write a hexa-side selftest in self/spawn_selftest.hexa that exercises spawn(`cat`)/send/recv_line/close, spawn(non-existent)/expect -1, spawn slow process/expect timeout, spawn 100 children/expect EMFILE around the cap. Exit gate: selftest passes; `./hexa run self/spawn_selftest.hexa` returns 0; valgrind shows zero leaked fds at exit.
rollout_plan.phase_B: Cutover anima_audio.hexa to use spawn() directly. Replace WORKER_CALL_SH bash invocation with: ensure_worker_handle() spawns python3 worker.py once; _run_py() encodes NDJSON inline (or via a stdlib JSON helper), send_line(h, request), recv_line(h, 20000), parse response. Delete _audio_worker_call.sh (133 lines) and the per-PPID FIFO/keepalive scaffolding. Behind ANIMA_AUDIO_WORKER_DIRECT=1 flag for one cycle of A/B comparison.
rollout_plan.phase_C: Re-run Phase 3 chain bench with ANIMA_AUDIO_WORKER_DIRECT=1. Target: chain warm mean ms ≤ 30 (from 148.6); 30-50× speedup on the 1177ms baseline. If <30× achieved, attribute the gap (likely WAV write or JSON encode/decode), file ω-runtime-2 follow-up. If ≥40× achieved, declare ω-audio-2 60× target met-with-asterisk and proceed to D.
rollout_plan.phase_D: Surface to other consumers. Candidates identified: (i) `hexa lsp` could swap exec_replace() for a persistent spawn() handle so the LSP doesn't restart on every JSON-RPC call; (ii) a persistent linter daemon for tool/r3_lint.py-class scripts; (iii) the omega_audio_emp probe runner; (iv) any anima cycle that calls python repeatedly (omega_cycle_atlas_ingest.hexa-style ingestion pipelines). Add convenience wrapper proc.spawn_json(cmd) in stdlib/proc.hexa for the NDJSON-per-line idiom.

fallback_options_if_primitive_too_costly.option: Unix domain socket (UDS) with worker as listener
fallback_options_if_primitive_too_costly.cost_to_implement: low (worker.py adds 20 lines using socketserver; hexa side uses existing exec_capture or curl/nc workaround)
fallback_options_if_primitive_too_costly.speedup_recovered: 20-40× (saves fork+FIFO open but still pays connect() syscall ~5-10ms per call)
fallback_options_if_primitive_too_costly.downside: still requires per-call connect; introduces socket-cleanup edge cases (stale .sock files); doesn't eliminate the bash shim, just shortens it
fallback_options_if_primitive_too_costly.verdict: viable middle ground if runtime change is rejected

fallback_options_if_primitive_too_costly.option: Inline interpreter — embed python via libpython into hexa runtime (or rewrite DSP in C-level numpy bindings)
fallback_options_if_primitive_too_costly.cost_to_implement: VERY HIGH (libpython embedding is a multi-week project; numpy C-API is unstable across versions; rewriting butter+lfilter+sosfilt in C is months of work and re-creates exactly the ctypes_shared_lib path the original design rejected)
fallback_options_if_primitive_too_costly.downside: scope creep; portability nightmare; segfaults take down hexa; binds hexa to a specific python version
fallback_options_if_primitive_too_costly.verdict: REJECTED — same conclusion as ω-audio-2 design's ctypes_shared_lib row

fallback_options_if_primitive_too_costly.downside: ω-audio-2 60× design target stays unmet on chains; harder to argue 'POC delivered the design promise' in retro
fallback_options_if_primitive_too_costly.verdict: acceptable if no other consumer needs persistent IPC; revisit only when a second consumer (LSP, linter) joins the queue

fallback_options_if_primitive_too_costly.option: Keep bash shim but optimize: (a) replace /bin/cat | /bin/base64 with a one-shot awk encoder; (b) keep FIFOs open via persistent reader inside helper; (c) move keepalive into the same shell process
fallback_options_if_primitive_too_costly.speedup_recovered: 15-25× (knocks ~30ms off the per-call floor by eliminating /bin/base64 fork)
fallback_options_if_primitive_too_costly.downside: still pays bash exec()-fork (~8ms × 1) per call; hits a ceiling of ~25-30ms per chain link; doesn't help future consumers
fallback_options_if_primitive_too_costly.verdict: viable as a Phase B-prime if Phase A primitive slips; documented for completeness

open_questions: OQ1: should recv_line return the line WITH or WITHOUT trailing newline? exec_stream returns WITH (matches fgets); we propose WITH for consistency, but JSON parsers strip whitespace anyway. Decision: WITH (matches existing exec_stream).

OQ2: should send() be line-buffered or unbuffered? Pipes are byte streams; we propose unbuffered raw write() — caller appends '\n' via send_line() helper if they want line framing. Worker-side python sys.stdin.readline() handles line buffering on its end.

OQ3: do we need a recv_chunk(h, max_bytes) primitive in v1, or is recv_line sufficient? For audio-worker NDJSON-per-line traffic, recv_line is sufficient. recv_chunk is a v2 follow-up gated on a second consumer needing it (LSP responses can be large).

OQ4: should the handle id be a TAG_INT or a fresh TAG_CHILD_HANDLE tag? TAG_INT is simpler (no hexa runtime tag-table change); the cost is that arithmetic on a handle is silently nonsensical. Decision: TAG_INT for v1; revisit if mistakes happen in practice.

OQ5: poll() vs select() for recv_line timeout? select() is in exec_capture already (more familiar to runtime.c readers); poll() is more correct for >1024 fds. v1 uses select() to match existing style; document the 1024 cap.
first_concrete_step_if_approved: Edit /Users/ghost/core/hexa-lang/self/runtime.c around line 6107 (immediately after hexa_exec_capture) to add the HexaChild struct, handle-table globals (g_children, g_children_cap, g_next_handle), and the seven C functions: hexa_spawn, hexa_send, hexa_send_line, hexa_recv_line, hexa_recv_status, hexa_alive, hexa_close. Then around line 6975 (after _hexa_init_exec_sha_fn_shims) add a _hexa_init_spawn_fn_shims() block registering the TAG_FN globals. Then in hexa_full.hexa around line 10230 (after exec_capture dispatch arm) add the 6 dispatch arms. Then add atexit(_hexa_child_atexit) call inside hexa_init_env (locate via grep). Expected diff: ~360 LOC across 2 files, no breaking changes to existing surface. Selftest harness self/spawn_selftest.hexa is ~100 LOC additional.

report_summary_for_caller.chosen_api_design: (a) handle-based: spawn(cmd) -> int, send/send_line/recv_line/recv_status/alive/close. Handle id is TAG_INT; never recycled within process lifetime. Multiple persistent children supported.
report_summary_for_caller.biggest_implementation_risk: B5 — pipe-write deadlock if a future consumer (not anima_audio) emits >4KB per line. Mitigation: v1 documents 'small-line protocols only', v2 adds recv_chunk(h, max_bytes). Secondary risk B1 — fd leak on missing close(); mitigated by atexit cleanup + soft cap + recommended with_spawn() helper.
report_summary_for_caller.projected_speedup_if_implemented: Per-call floor drops from 67-75ms to ~7-8ms (eliminates bash exec ~8ms, FIFO open/close ~25ms, /bin/base64 fork ~30-40ms). Chain (2 links) drops from 148.6ms warm to ~30ms warm → 30-50× speedup over 1177ms baseline. Approaches the 60× design target; remaining gap to 60× is WAV I/O + JSON framing, addressable separately.
